steps.title: Identify the contrast signal
steps.content: The passage shifts from one idea (particles enter Earth's atmosphere at high speed) to a contrasting one (relatively few of these particles reach Earth's surface). The word “however” marks this contrast. The dependent clause starting with “since” then explains why few reach the surface—they burn up due to air compression.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule for parenthetical transition words
steps.content:
When a transitional word like “however” appears within a sentence to show a contrast or qualification, it must be set off by commas on both sides: “IC, however, DC.” This structure allows the contrast to be noted while the dependent clause following “since” explains the reason.

The other three choices violate this rule: a colon doesn't precede a parenthetical word (A), a period creates a sentence boundary that leaves a dependent clause stranded as a fragment (C), and a semicolon and comma together don't correctly enclose a parenthetical word (D).

That matches choice B — B is correct.
